coredata.dc:title: Protein adsorption on magnesium and its alloys: A review
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Höhn, Sarah
Virtanen, Sannakaisa
Boccaccini, Aldo R.
coredata.dc:description: The initial interaction of biomaterials with the biological environment is based on the adsorption of proteins on the surface and the interaction with ions and water molecules to form various reactive interfaces. Understanding the protein adsorption mechanisms, kinetics, and thermodynamics is essential to improve the design of new biocompatible materials and to control protein interaction with solid surfaces. Magnesium and its alloys are promising materials for biodegradable metallic devices due to several advantages, including reduced stress shielding effect in the case of orthopedic implants, and removing the requirement of a second surgery for implant removal. Appropriate protein adsorption on magnesium and Mg alloys is essential for applications in bone tissue regeneration and for the effective integration of Mg implants. In this review, we summarize and discuss previous studies on magnesium-protein interactions, including the adsorption of proteins on Mg surfaces as well as the influence of proteins on Mg corrosion. In most cases, the influence of protein adsorption on magnesium corrosion was determined using polarization curves, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The results show inhibited degradation due to insoluble salt formation (barrier layer) or formation of unstable and porous layers followed by higher corrosion rates depending on the substrate (cpMg or alloys) and/or media. A comparison of the use of different solutions on surface reactions (with and without proteins) is presented and the influence of corrosion layer formation is discussed. In this review, the influence of substrate and media on corrosion mechanism and protein adsorption on magnesium and magnesium alloys is highlighted, which significantly affects the in-vivo and in-vitro corrosion behavior, being thus of high relevance for the application of Mg and Mg alloys in medicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium
Adsorption
Proteins
Interface
Corrosion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: 2.2 Interaction between calcium-phosphate layers and proteins

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical composition of the CaP layer formed on magnesium during immersion in DMEM for 1, 3, and 5 days by means of XPS; (a) at room temperature; (b) in the incubator at 37 °C (5% CO2 and 95% humidity); (c) in DMEM + 20% FCS in the incubator; (d) calculated Ca2p/P2p ratios. Reprinted from [83] with permission of Elsevier.

Optical images of the Mg-Nd-Zn-Zr surface after 7 days of immersion in (A) M199, (B) M199 + FBS and (C) after HUVEC coculture. The corresponding corrosion rate measurements and the XPS survey spectra are shown in (D) and (E). Reprinted from [85] with permission of Elsevier.
Schematic representation of degradation process of M1A magnesium alloy (a) in SBF (b–d) compared to SBF + albumin (e–g). Reprinted from [59] with permission of Elsevier.
Calcium phosphate phases and the Ca/P ratio [88,89].
Typical polymer-inorganic systems used to coat Mg and Mg alloys.
Comparison of results of protein adsorption on magnesium obtained by different substrates and media.

head.ce:title: Protein adsorption on magnesium and its alloys: A review

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Surface Science and Corrosion, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Erlangen-Nuremberg, Martensstr. 7, 91058 Erlangen, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomaterials, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Erlangen-Nuremberg, Cauerstr. 6, 91058 Erlangen, Germany

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The initial interaction of biomaterials with the biological environment is based on the adsorption of proteins on the surface and the interaction with ions and water molecules to form various reactive interfaces. Understanding the protein adsorption mechanisms, kinetics, and thermodynamics is essential to improve the design of new biocompatible materials and to control protein interaction with solid surfaces. Magnesium and its alloys are promising materials for biodegradable metallic devices due to several advantages, including reduced stress shielding effect in the case of orthopedic implants, and removing the requirement of a second surgery for implant removal. Appropriate protein adsorption on magnesium and Mg alloys is essential for applications in bone tissue regeneration and for the effective integration of Mg implants. In this review, we summarize and discuss previous studies on magnesium-protein interactions, including the adsorption of proteins on Mg surfaces as well as the influence of proteins on Mg corrosion. In most cases, the influence of protein adsorption on magnesium corrosion was determined using polarization curves, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The results show inhibited degradation due to insoluble salt formation (barrier layer) or formation of unstable and porous layers followed by higher corrosion rates depending on the substrate (cpMg or alloys) and/or media. A comparison of the use of different solutions on surface reactions (with and without proteins) is presented and the influence of corrosion layer formation is discussed. In this review, the influence of substrate and media on corrosion mechanism and protein adsorption on magnesium and magnesium alloys is highlighted, which significantly affects the in-vivo and in-vitro corrosion behavior, being thus of high relevance for the application of Mg and Mg alloys in medicine.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnesium and Mg alloys are being increasingly considered as an essential component of medical technology research. The focus is on screws and plates for bone fixation, tissue engineering scaffolds, as well as on cardiovascular stents [1–4]. Since the light metal is a trace element in the human body, Mg is considered to be biocompatible [5–7]. A major problem in the area of bone fixation is the difference in the Young’s moduli of conventional metallic biomaterials and human bone. Titanium, CoCr alloys, and stainless steels have Young’s moduli higher than that of human bone, resulting in the so-called “stress-shielding” [8]. In contrast and compared to the aforementioned metals, magnesium has an elastic modulus of 45 GPa, e.g. close to that of bone, and it has better mechanical properties compared to ceramics and polymers [8–10]. Biodegradable implants based on Mg provide also the possibility to maintain stability over a few weeks to months to guarantee the healing of the tissue until the implant has completely dissolved [11–13].

Among these aforementioned surface modifications, MAO (microarc oxidation) coatings are prepared in different solutions to form highly adherent ceramic (oxide) coatings with high hardness, and good wear and corrosion resistance. Zeng et al. [32] fabricated porous MAO/PLLA composite coatings by means of dip-coating and freeze-drying. The MAO/PLLA coating significantly enhanced corrosion resistance and a stable and appropriate pH value was maintained in HBSS due to neutralization of acidic and alkaline corrosion products. Investigations by Yu et al. [33] showed good results in terms of reduction of bacteria growth on MAO/chitosan coatings but the composite did not achieve the expected corrosion protection efficiency.

Despite substantial efforts in developing coatings of different compositions for Mg and Mg alloys, and the fact that interesting in-vitro and in-vivo results have been reported in literature, the fundamental interaction of Mg alloys (coated and uncoated) with proteins remains a subject for further in-depth investigations.

The adsorption of proteins on biomaterial surfaces plays a major role in determining the interaction between implants and tissues [34]. Proteins will be spontaneously adsorbed onto biomaterial surfaces, followed by cellular attachment, proliferation, and migration [35,36]. Hence, understanding protein adsorption mechanisms and the factors influencing them is important to design biocompatible materials and devices. In blood plasma, the adsorption is the result of a competition between protein diffusion and strength of protein-surface interaction. Due to different protein sizes and affinity of proteins to the surface, the initial adsorption is dominated by the smaller and mobile proteins (e.g. albumin), which are later replaced by the larger proteins like fibrinogen (Vroman effect) [37]. Proteins can also change their conformation upon adsorption [36]. Indeed, the kinetics and thermodynamics of protein conformational changes are very complex and the understanding is in its early stages. Some studies have assumed that the adsorption kinetics is determined by the diffusion of the protein to the surface [38], whereas others assume it is controlled by a kinetic and activated process [39,40]. The protein adsorption behavior is further complicated in the case of biodegradable or bioreactive surfaces such as in Mg and Mg alloys.

In this review, we primarily focus on protein adsorption on magnesium and Mg alloys. Based on a comprehensive literature review, the parameters that determine the different adsorption processes and the effect of conformational changes on Mg are discussed. In particular, chemical composition of the surface, surface roughness, surface hydrophilicity and wettability, as well as the effect of the composition of the media are discussed. An intensive knowledge of protein adsorption on magnesium is not only beneficial to optimize the surface structure of implants and scaffolds but also to expand specific applications of Mg and Mg alloys in the field of biomedicine.

Proteins adsorb in differing quantities, densities, conformations, and orientations, depending on the chemical and physical characteristics of the surface. Protein adsorption on solid surfaces is complex involving van der Waals, hydrophobic, and electrostatic interactions, as well as hydrogen bonding. Properties of the proteins [41], substrates and media [42,43], such as the pH, and the protein-substrate interactions at the molecular scale, including surface roughness [44], have been shown to play a fundamental role in protein adsorption. Proteins tend to adhere more strongly to non-polar than to polar surfaces, to high surface tension than to low, and to charged than to uncharged surfaces [45].

The aforementioned properties of the substrate and media include, for example, the chemical composition [46], dissolution or degradation behavior of the substrate and pH value of the medium [47–49], microstructure of the materials, as well as hydrophobicity [50], crystallinity [51], surface roughness [52], and reactivity of the surface [53]. In general, the surface of synthetic biomaterials (e.g., polymers, metals, and ceramics) are not bioactive themselves, but proteins that adsorb to the biomaterial surface following exposure to biological fluids can enhance the surface bioactivity. Exceptions are such materials known to react in biological fluids, such as bioactive glasses, some biopolymers and Mg/Mg alloys. In the case of Mg/Mg alloys the interaction with proteins present in the biological medium will have a notable effect on the corrosion behavior of the substrate. Johnson et al. [54] demonstrated the inhibition or promoting effect of serum proteins on magnesium degradation depending on the type of proteins and properties of the magnesium surfaces. Their investigations reveal that chelation of metal ions and oxides by proteins can cause higher degradation rates of MgY. In the case of cpMg, serum proteins inhibited the degradation. This is in agreement with the study of Li et al. [55]. In the initial stages of immersion in Hanḱs solution with bovine serum albumin (BSA), the protein adsorption can inhibit the corrosion of Mg-1.5Zn-0.6Zr-0.2Sc. With longer immersion time, BSA shows chelation effects and can accelerate the corrosion processes. The effects of inorganic and organic components of biological fluids on the degradation of pure magnesium were also investigated by Yamamoto et al. [56]. They suggested that the amino acids in E-MEM (Eaglés minimum essential medium) accelerate magnesium corrosion by chelating metal cations to the organic components, while the inhibition effect of the proteins in E-MEM + FBS is caused by protein adsorption followed by the formation of insoluble salt layers on the magnesium surface. This formed salt layer can act as an effective barrier against corrosion and is influenced by the type of solution used for the experiments. Time-lapse degradation characteristics of pure Mg were analyzed by Zeng et al. in artificial saliva [57]. The results demonstrated the formation of a dense degradation layer, which consists of calcium phosphate and a thin magnesium hydrate layer close to the substrate. The degradation is time-dependent, so after 10 days of immersion, the calcium phosphate layer was delaminated and the inner magnesium hydrate layer was exposed. Harandi et al. [58] presented research about albumin adsorption and its influence on stress corrosion cracking of AZ91D magnesium alloy in Hanḱs solution. They compared the corrosion resistance in the presence and in the absence of albumin, observing an initial increase in corrosion resistance in presence of proteins, while at a later stage the decrease in resistance was attributed to the chelation of amino acids with the corrosion products. This is in agreement with the study of Wang et al. [59].

The general experimental findings on metal oxides show the affinity of proteins to hydrophobic surfaces due to different surface chemical compositions [60]. One exception is the adsorption of glycoproteins, which adsorb extensively on hydrophilic planar surfaces [61]. The corrosion rate of pure magnesium increases with the glucose content in physiological saline, because glucose rapidly transforms into gluconic acid, which decreases the solution pH and therefore attacks the oxidation film on the surface [62,63]. The results by Wang et al. [64] demonstrated that amino acid and glucose inhibited the corrosion of pure magnesium, while in the presence of both amino acid and glucose the corrosion rate is accelerated in saline solution. Functional groups and bonding between amino acid and glucose restrict the formation of the protective Mg(OH)2 precipitates. The effect of anions in saline solution was investigated by Zeng et al. [65]. The results showed that in the presence of bicarbonate and sulphate ions the corrosion rate is accelerated, whereas in the presence of hydrogen phosphate and dihydrogen phosphate the open-circuit potential is decreased and the corrosion rate significantly reduced. Further investigations were made to compare the influence of Tris-HCl and Tris on the corrosion of AZ31 alloy [66]. Hydrogen evolution and corrosion density were one order of magnitude higher in the presence of Tris-HCl and the formation of precipitates (HCO3 –, SO4 2−, HPO4 2−, and H2PO4 −) was prevented. Corrosion behavior of AZ31 alloy in saline solution with different glucose concentrations and in the presence or absence of Tris was systematically investigated by Li et al. [67]. The influence of glucose depends on the possibility to transform it to gluconic acid. Glucose protected the Mg surface, while gluconic acid was shown to accelerate Mg dissolution. The presence of Tris also accelerated the transformation of glucose to gluconic acid due to alkaline conditions followed by general corrosion and/or localized corrosion.

In comparison, the systematic and fundamental study of Kirkland et al. [68] reveals the increase in corrosion rate by calcium addition to the alloy, but also shows that the presence of proteins principally affects the anodic reaction kinetics, reducing the rate of metallic dissolution.

Liu et al. [69] investigated the corrosion susceptibility of magnesium alloys in different simulated body fluids. The addition of BSA changed the open-circuit potential to more positive values significantly leading to decreased corrosion reactions. Moreover, a higher BSA concentration in solution also decreased the corrosion processes. The time-dependent influence of BSA on the corrosion of magnesium rare-earth alloy was investigated by Rettig et al., indicating an initial inhibition of corrosion by albumin, followed by acceleration for longer immersion times [70]. The corrosion degradation of Mg-Ca alloy has also been studied by Liu et al. [71]; hydrogen gas collection and electrochemical techniques were used to study the in vitro corrosion degradation behavior in the presence of albumin. The significant amounts of Mg2+ produced during Mg corrosion can interact with the negatively charged albumin molecules resulting in rapid protein adsorption on the magnesium surface. In addition, the diffusion of Mg2+ into solution during corrosion leads to the formation of an Mg-protein complex resulting in reduced free binding sites for protein adsorption. With changing the concentration and hence diffusion of relating ions such as Mg2+, the surface layer formation is also strongly influenced [56]. Different shapes of adsorbed proteins due to their high flexibility have been reported [56]. Hence, the transfer and diffusion of ions from the solution to the magnesium surface can be inhibited by protein adsorption layers there by influencing the corrosion resistance in a positive way. Nevertheless, Mueller et al. [72] showed that albumin-containing PBS solutions form unstable and porous protein layers at high concentrations with high diffusion processes and higher magnesium dissolution. Hence, differences in corrosion properties of magnesium were found even in the same simulated body fluids and at the same purity levels and differ in different treatment conditions or under different experimental conditions. Higher or lower chloride concentrations, the absence or presence of phosphates, and the absence or presence of proteins should be always taken into account for testing in vitro magnesium and its alloys [72]. Pure magnesium degrades faster in NaCl solution followed by E-MEM, but protein adsorption and insoluble salt formation decelerate the degradation, whereas organic compounds like amino acids accelerate the dissolution [56]. Surface hydrophilicity has been widely used to characterize the surface wettability of biomaterials. It is generally well known that a hydrophobic surface will more strongly adsorb proteins than a neutrally charged hydrophilic surface due to the attraction of the nonpolar parts of the protein molecules toward the surface. In the case of bovine serum albumin, the adsorption and desorption mechanisms are generally different on hydrophilic and hydrophobic surfaces due to different conformation and orientation [73]. It is reported that linker molecules provide better protein adsorption [74]. Silanization by aminopropyl-triethoxysilane (APTES) and ascorbic acid (VitC) have been used to functionalize magnesium surfaces and to act as linker for further protein adsorption. Compared to the APTES and VitC self-assembled monolayers by Killian et al. [74], N,N-carbonyldiimidazole (CDI) and stearic acid (SA) were investigated by Wagener et al. [75]. Contact angles between 5° and 50° were measured. In addition, the ratio of peak intensities were calculated by means of ToF-SIMS after the BSA coating. The values indicated that more BSA can attach to the more hydrophobic (APTES + VitC, SA) compared to the more hydrophilic CDI-functionalized surfaces.

Protein based coatings on Mg were developed by Bowen et al. [76]. The degradation rates of iron and magnesium wires with and without a fibrin coating were evaluated in a cell-culture medium. The authors showed localized corrosion of iron and more uniformly corrosion of magnesium, which can not be positively changed by the fibrin coating. Electrochemical investigations by Wagener et al. [77] showed the influence of bovine serum albumin and lysozyme on iron and magnesium corrosion. A strong corrosion-inhibiting effect of BSA on magnesium and slight accelerated corrosion on iron were measured. It was suggested [77], that the BSA adsorption is favoured on magnesium due to strong electrostatic interactions between the negatively charged BSA and the positively charged magnesium surface. However, for Fe oxide/hydroxide a stronger electrostatic attraction is expected for lysozyme. These findings are in general relevant and showed the significant effect of surface charge and cation-protein binding strength on protein adsorption.

The relationship between the wettability and protein adsorption has been also examined by Yamamato et al. [78]. Protein adsorption measurements on cpMg and AZ61 reveal higher BSA and lower fibronectin adsorption at a water contact angle of 60°. However, the water contact angle measured on AZ80 was also around 60° but showed the lowest protein adsorption for both fibronectin and albumin. In the case of AZ31 alloy, the water contact angle was around 40° and showed lower fibronectin adsorption and higher BSA adsorption compared to cpMg. Therefore, the protein adsorption is not simply controlled by surface wettability and other surface properties as chemical composition and surface charge should be taken into account, as discussed before.

The above summary highlights that the effect of surface properties and composition of the media on protein adsorption on Mg and Mg alloys has started to be discussed in detail and it is being fundamentally investigated by different approaches and characterization techniques. Summarizing the research described above (Table 3 ), it is clear that depending on the type of alloying elements, surface treatments and time of protein adsorption, magnesium corrosion can be significantly influenced. Surface modifications to more hydrophobic surfaces can promote protein adsorption followed by a change of the kinetically favored orientation to a more stable protein conformation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2.2 Interaction between calcium-phosphate layers and proteins
ce:section.ce:para: The research on the corrosion of magnesium in physiological environments is focused on the corrosion layer and degradation rates. In-vitro studies in simulated body fluid (SBF) or cell culture medium have shown that the corrosion layer formed during immersion consists of amorphous calcium phosphate (CaP) [79–82]. This type of interactions is relevant when Mg and Mg alloy implants or scaffolds become in contact with bone tissue. Analysis of the chemical composition during immersion in DMEM (Dulbeccós Modified Eagle Medium) at room temperature and in the incubator at 37 °C (5% CO2, 95% humidity) have shown similar Ca2p and P2p signals by means of XPS (Fig. 1 ) [83]. This corrosion layer can enhance corrosion resistance of the substrate, and is influenced by the presence of proteins. For example, the addition of 20% FCS to the immersion solution was observed to simultaneously decelerate the CaP formation and to lead to the incorporation of magnesium in the layer and therefore to a change in the Ca2p/P2p ratio.

The ionic radii of Mg2+ is significantly smaller compared to Ca2+, so the ion substitution in the HA lattice leads to an increase of the interplanar distance between calcium ions due to crystal lattice distortion and reduction of the lateral electrostatic repulsion. Hence, this substitution can increase the electrostatic interactions between HA and BSA [84]. XPS analysis by Zhang et al. [85] also indicate the formation of insoluble hydroxyapatite and tricalcium phosphate on the surface of Mg-Nd-Zn-Zr as well as incorporation of proteins in the corrosion layer. They also reported a decreased in-vitro corrosion rate and no corrosion pits were observed on the surface by serum addition into the electrolyte (Fig. 2 ).

Since the surface is not completely covered with proteins or the adsorbed protein layer is not dense, localized corrosion occurs. However, with increasing immersion time, albumin will be rapidly adsorbed around this area followed by effective suppression of corrosion rate as shown by Wang et al. [59]. In the case of M1A alloy (Mg-Mn alloy), the degradation is quite uniform compared to that in SBF without proteins (Fig. 3 , [59]). It is also well reported that albumin undergoes a neutral-acidic transition and becomes negatively charged (isoelectric pH 4.7–4.9) at physiological pH of 7.4. Therefore, the adsorption on the CaP surface is mainly determined by the interaction of the negative carboxylate group with the positive Ca sites, and by binding the positive amino groups to negative P/OH sites. In addition, several calcium phosphate coatings, including hydroxyapatite (HA), octacalcium phosphate (OCP) and brushite (DCPD) have been investigated previously to increase the biocompatibility and corrosion-resistance of Mg. The complex interactions between proteins and the CaP surface are also influenced by physicochemical properties (etc. roughness, porosity, and charge), functional groups and protein conformation. Luo et al. [86] described the protein adsorption on hydroxyapatite as cooperative. This cooperativity is different for different proteins and solutions. With increased concentration of surface proteins, the protein-protein distance is reduced followed by an increased lateral interaction. The now attractive forces lead to a change in the conformation and therefore to an increase in cooperativity. With regard to the aforementioned previously published results [83,86], the effect of proteins on the nucleation and growth of calcium phosphates as well as the effect of the calcium phosphate coatings on protein adsorption are fundamentally different. Proteins may delay the crystal growth process of CaP due to their presence in solution. Hence, this results in the inhibition of crystal growth or growth at slower rates followed by modification of the calcium phosphate morphology and Ca2p/P2p ratios. This is attributed to the capacity of the proteins to complex the calcium ions by their – carboxylate groups (COO). On the contrary, protein adsorption on calcium phosphate surfaces is dominated by electrostatic forces. The charged COO– and NH3 + are the critical groups that interact with the CaP surface. Addadi et al. [87] showed that acidic proteins like albumin preferentially adsorbed on CaP crystals due specific electrostatic interactions.

The results from the literature show that proteins in solution can act as promoter, inhibitor or both, depending on the time scale, of magnesium and Mg alloy corrosion. This review has revealed the differences in protein adsorption behavior that occur depending on the system used, the range of protein concentration, protein size, protein-surface interactions (surface charge), media, and protein-protein interactions. In addition, immersion of magnesium in simulated body fluids leads to the formation of a corrosion product layer on the surface (conversion layer). This layer consists of calcium phosphate and leads to decreasing corrosion rates. With regard to this behaviour, protein adsorption on calcium phosphate surfaces like hydroxyapatite coatings is different compared to adsorption in the presence of proteins and calcium phosphate compounds in solution simultaneously. Protein adsorption is a complex phenomenon due to the variety of protein conformation, protein-protein as well as protein-surface interactions and the factors that will influence the physico-chemical characteristics. Based on the available results, it can be concluded that knowledge about protein adsorption on magnesium and all the influencing factors has been generated by dedicated research efforts worldwide in the last years. This body of knowledge is beneficial to optimize the surface properties of implants and scaffolds to expand specific applications of Mg and Mg alloys and to control corrosion behavior in the field of biomedicine. Future research should explore further the interaction of cell adhesive proteins on specific Mg/Mg alloy surfaces and the effect of CaP formation, especially in long-term in-vitro and in-vivo studies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge funding from DFG (German Science Foundation) through the Cluster of Excellence “Engineering of Advanced Materials (EAM)”, project EXC 315, at University of Erlangen-Nuremberg.
